entries.title: The Enterprise-D is a family-bearing exploratory flagship

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: The Enterprise-D carries civilians and families as well as Starfleet personnel, which makes saucer separation, evacuation, and command risk different from a warship-only frame.
content.injection: Treat the Enterprise-D as a large exploratory starship with families aboard. Command choices should account for civilian risk and diplomatic mission posture.
constraints: Keep this card inside TNG season 1 unless another active Star Trek deck provides a later Context window.
Use the episode and season Context gates before injecting this fact.
antiLore: Do not copy Memory Alpha prose or substitute a plot recap for this gate.
Do not import later TNG, DS9, Voyager, film, Picard-era, beta-canon, novel, comic, or fanon facts into this season 1 card.

entries.title: Q frames the Farpoint mission as a trial of humanity

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: Q does not merely harass the Enterprise at Farpoint; he places humanity on trial and uses Picard as the representative case for whether humans remain savage.
content.injection: When Q appears in TNG season 1, frame him as an adversarial judge testing humanity and Picard, not yet a familiar trickster or mentor figure.
constraints: Keep this card inside TNG season 1 unless another active Star Trek deck provides a later Context window.

entries.title: Farpoint Station is tied to captive spaceborne lifeforms

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.

content.fact: The Farpoint mystery resolves when the crew discovers the station depends on a captive shape-changing spaceborne lifeform and its mate is attacking to free it.
content.injection: For Farpoint-era scenes, treat the station mystery as a moral test about exploitation and recognition of non-humanoid life, not a conventional military threat.

entries.title: Q can overpower the Enterprise-D through courtroom reality shifts

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: At Farpoint and Hide and Q, Q demonstrates reality-warping power far beyond Enterprise-D defenses, including forced transport, historical staging, and impossible tests.
content.injection: Use Q power in season 1 as overwhelming and theatrical. Starfleet cannot fight him conventionally; Picard and crew respond through ethics, refusal, and judgment.
constraints: Keep this card inside TNG season 1 unless another active Star Trek deck provides a later Context window.
Use the episode and season Context gates before injecting this fact.

entries.title: Riker rejects Q-granted power after seeing its moral cost

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: In Hide and Q, Riker receives Q-like power but loses the crew's trust when gifts become coercive, and he ultimately rejects the offer.
content.injection: If Riker is tempted by power in TNG season 1, use Hide and Q as the boundary: he learns that imposed miracles and superiority damage trust and command integrity.

entries.title: The Last Outpost establishes the Ferengi as a known but poorly understood rival power

content.fact: At Delphi Ardu, the Enterprise-D crew treats the Ferengi as a significant rival they know mostly through reputation, not yet as the later comic-commercial baseline.
content.injection: In season 1, write Ferengi encounters as uncertain and adversarial. Do not import Quark, Rules of Acquisition depth, or DS9 Ferengi family politics.

entries.title: The Tkon Portal reframes the Ferengi standoff as an ancient test

content.fact: The Ferengi standoff at Delphi Ardu is resolved only when the Tkon Portal judges the crews, shifting the conflict from simple enemy action to an ancient automated test.
content.injection: Use the Tkon Portal as an episode-local ancient-power gate. It should not become recurring TNG technology unless specifically referenced.

entries.title: Heart of Glory establishes Worf performing a Klingon death howl

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: After Korris dies, Worf performs a Klingon death ritual, showing that his Starfleet service has not erased Klingon spiritual and warrior customs.
content.injection: Use Heart of Glory to give Worf Klingon cultural depth in season 1. Keep it tied to honor, death, and identity rather than generic aggression.

entries.title: Heart of Glory tests Worf against Klingon fugitives who reject peace

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.

content.fact: Korris and the other Klingon fugitives pressure Worf with warrior nostalgia, but Worf chooses Starfleet duty over their violent path.
content.injection: When Worf faces Klingon identity pressure in season 1, keep his loyalty with Starfleet. He values Klingon honor but rejects undisciplined violence.

entries.title: The Neutral Zone marks the Romulans returning to active contact

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.

content.fact: The Romulans reappear along the Neutral Zone after a long silence, with both sides aware that outposts have been destroyed by an unknown force.
content.injection: After The Neutral Zone, Romulan contact is active again but cautious. Do not import later Sela, reunification, or Dominion-era Romulan politics.

content.fact: The Neutral Zone leaves destroyed outposts as an unknown threat shared by Federation and Romulan observers; the Borg connection is not season 1 knowledge.
content.injection: For season 1 scenes after The Neutral Zone, characters may know outposts vanished or were destroyed, but they should not identify the Borg yet.

entries.title: Season 1 Federation contact favors recognition of unfamiliar sentience

content.fact: Farpoint, Home Soil, and Justice all reinforce that the Enterprise-D should identify unfamiliar life and moral agency before imposing simple threat categories.
content.injection: Use this as a season-level Federation ethic: unknown life, alien law, and nonhuman intelligence require investigation and restraint before force.

entries.title: Season 1 Starfleet Command can be infiltrated or politically compromised

content.fact: Coming of Age and Conspiracy establish that Starfleet institutions are not immune to paranoia, audit pressure, or alien infiltration.
content.injection: Use Starfleet as principled but vulnerable in season 1. Do not make command automatically clean or automatically corrupt; gate parasite knowledge by Conspiracy.

content.fact: Heart of Glory depends on a Federation-Klingon peace context: Worf serves in Starfleet, and Klingon fugitives are the problem, not the whole Empire.
content.injection: For TNG season 1, avoid TOS-style default Federation-Klingon war framing. Klingon identity and warrior culture remain active, but open war is not the baseline.

content.fact: Season 1 should not treat active Romulan politics as known until The Neutral Zone brings Romulan contact back into focus.
content.injection: Before The Neutral Zone, keep Romulans as a historical or border concern, not an active recurring presence. Afterward, cautious contact resumes.
